item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think his website content needs more (some) errant apostrophes, and more unnecessary ALL CAPS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great. I really loved the addendum. Keep writing interviews, they are very engaging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of the dihydrogen monoxide scare things, good stuff

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are only so many URL suffixes to try. I think you should blackbox fluorine instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you explain to me what happened here? Did they turn his 30 buddies into fluorine and bury em and turn him into this or what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, okay, did that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the Washington Post had something specifically on this, in fact: Sand towers. In the print edition it had a photo specifically of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: His website URL should be blackboxed or excluded or something. Just in case some bozo ever actually uses it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my new favourites.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They pump the air from the airlock outside when they cycle it. The Foundation aren't idiots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The containment cell is to be outfitted with an airlock to prevent contamination of the rest of the facility.

But air from the cell would get into the airlock, which would then get out into the site.

Besides, I get to know I was right all along. How many people get to see behind the veil of lies, man?

The government is conspiring to turn everyone into animate, sapient Teflon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation didn't instantly contain him. Even if they could contain a guy containing dioxygen difluoride without him exploding, which I'm not all sure of, they certainly couldn't have found him before he exploded. I wanted to have it in there, but I couldn't make it make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or, he'd be one of the most dangerous things at the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That may actually be something real they have at the McMillan Water Treatment Plant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sand towers.

Sand towers.

It's so incredibly, yet realistically off-the-wall bizarre and I just can't get over that simple two-word phrase. What the hell does that even mean? It's brilliant!

As for the article itself, this guy has a really fun personality, and overall it works in a way I can't quite put my finger on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hrrrm. Okay… Well. I think a lot of this could have been written more simply, and would have improved the article. And much of the article's content feels like dead weight; it either doesn't add anything or isn't interesting/amusing enough.

But all that said, the use of UIU and all of the last interview log (which is funny and very nicely done) pushes me to an upvote. This article is actually a pretty good example of the value of ending on a high note.

EDIT: On second read, I should be clearer -

The first paragraph is really the worst in terms of 'dead weight'. It feels like anti-reader kryptonite. It may be in-universe useful, but it's not interesting to read and difficult to absorb.

Beyond that, the article could be improved by splitting up paragraphs for readability. Notably, splitting the 2nd Containment Procedures paragraph, and splitting the final Description paragraph (between " Addendum 1862-10." and "Of possible note").

I didn't feel I got enough out of the website logs, but they read as authentic & others appear to like them, so take that critique with a grain of salt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You made a UIU skip. ♥

I love the conspiracy theorist spin, too. The logs are great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's fair. I'll change that, since I use UIU later anyhow, and some people didn't know the acronym.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, but I think it would be worthwhile to actually spell out "in custody of the FBI's Unusual Incident Unit" in the containment procedures rather than just going with "UIU".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, no. Nothing of the sort. Although his buddy is really with the UIU. Hm. I don't think I can easily fit his perspective on what happened to him in the article, and I'm not sure that would clarify what really happened, and even then, I don't want to give everything away, but I may edit it at some point to clear things up a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*Starts reading containment procedures*
"Wait, aren't all of those substances that are safe around-"
*Gets to calcium gluconate gel*
"OH GOD IT'S A FLOURINE MONSTER"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure he went to go investigate the weird vats his buddies at UIU were dealing with and an XM-class supervillain origin story event occurred.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are hints, though I'll try to make it a bit clearer. What happened to him is rather different from what happened to the readers of his site that went missing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview log and the bits from the website really made up my mind on this one. The website bits are perfect. They really do have the ring of a real conspiracy website. Would be nice to have kind of a hint as to what made the guy the way he is though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh FOOF, how horrid you are!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, this was inspired by the XKCD thing. Didn't think O2F2 would fit here, because if he had any of it in him, he wouldn't be here any more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was kind of hoping for the involvement of dioxygen difluoride after xkcd made me aware yesterday of its terrifying, terrifying existence.

The bit about the coupons was awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really fond of the technobabble in this, because it isn't even technobabble.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not poorly written, but it really didn't make me feel anything. I just did not care about this.

I do, however, like the idea of someone thinking they're being held by the UIU in this way, but it's not enough to save it from a downvote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it. Sometimes the world is really out to get you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My take on the dangerous humanoid SCP whose containment procedures have us giving it stuff it asks for. Now, the reason this guy knows some stuff it seems he really oughtn't is because most groups aren't as secretive as the Foundation (in my headcanon, the UIU is mentioned on the FBI's web site, although not their mission). Moreover, he's wrong about a number of things, like in his conflation of the SH with the Fifthists. Exactly how wrong is he about the other stuff? Well, I'm not saying just yet.
